title: update CI images to include pasta
body:
Ref: https://github.com/containers/automation_images/pull/246

<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

In case you're only changing docs, make sure to prefix the pull-request title with "[CI:DOCS]". That will prevent functional tests from running and save time and energy.

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name. Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

<!--
If no, just write `None` in the release-note block below. If yes, a release note
is required: Enter your extended release note in the block below. If the PR
requires additional action from users switching to the new release, include the
string "action required".

For more information on release notes, please follow the Kubernetes model:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/release-notes.md
-->

```release-note
None
```

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *cevich*, *Luap99* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [Luap99] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

FYI these are the installed versions:
```
Fedora release 37 (Thirty Seven)
Kernel:  6.0.8-300.fc37.x86_64
Cgroups:  cgroup2fs
aardvark-dns-1.2.0-6.fc37-x86_64
conmon-2.1.4-3.fc37-x86_64
containernetworking-plugins-1.1.1-8.fc37-x86_64
containers-common-1-73.fc37-noarch
container-selinux-2.191.0-1.fc37-noarch
criu-3.17.1-3.fc37-x86_64
crun-1.7-1.fc37-x86_64
golang-1.19.2-1.fc37-x86_64
libseccomp-2.5.3-3.fc37-x86_64
netavark-1.2.0-5.fc37-x86_64
passt-0^20221110.g4129764-1.fc37-x86_64
podman-4.3.1-1.fc37-x86_64
runc-1.1.4-1.fc37-x86_64
skopeo-1.10.0-3.fc37-x86_64
slirp4netns-1.2.0-8.fc37-x86_64
```
@giuseppe reping, could please take a look at the e2e failures, they look all cgroup related.
it looks like a regression in crun 1.7.  Taking a look...

fix here: https://github.com/containers/crun/pull/1084

I'll release 1.7.2 ASAP

> fix here: [containers/crun#1084](https://github.com/containers/crun/pull/1084)
> 
> I'll release 1.7.2 ASAP

Thank you.

rpms here:

- https://bodhi.fedoraproject.org/updates/FEDORA-2022-7e7bce3425
- https://bodhi.fedoraproject.org/updates/FEDORA-2022-87411cbdd0

@giuseppe @adrianreber It looks like checkpoint/restore is broken on fedora 36

Error is
```
could not load `libcriu.so.2`
```
These are the version installed:
```
Kernel:  6.0.12-200.fc36.x86_64
criu-3.17.1-2.fc36-x86_64
crun-1.7.2-1.fc36-x86_64
```
How to fix this?

> @giuseppe @adrianreber It looks like checkpoint/restore is broken on fedora 36
> 
> Error is
> 
> ```
> could not load `libcriu.so.2`
> ```
> 
> These are the version installed:
> 
> ```
> Kernel:  6.0.12-200.fc36.x86_64
> criu-3.17.1-2.fc36-x86_64
> crun-1.7.2-1.fc36-x86_64
> ```
> 
> How to fix this?

`dnf -y install criu-libs`

The `crun` fix is on its way: https://bodhi.fedoraproject.org/updates/FEDORA-2022-68051670ee

I have an [older image-update PR](https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/16692) I've been kicking around for a while now.  Since this one is better, and there's no sense in two separate image-update PRs, I'll close mine in favor of this one.  I'll keep subscribed here though in case my eyeballs are needed.
For reference, test failures are caused by a bind() kernel bug: https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2159066
@n1hility hey, could you look at the windows smoke test failure? It looks like podman machine init times out without any info printed.

Timings:
| type | distro              | user     |    local |   remote | container |
| ---- | --------            | ----     |    ----: |   -----: | --------: |
| int  | fedora-36           | root     |    28:30 |    34:49 |     31:31 |
| int  | fedora-37           | root     |    24:41 |    29:50 |     25:20 |
| int  | fedora-36           | rootless |    29:59 |          |
| int  | fedora-37           | rootless |    26:28 |          |
| sys  | fedora-36           | root     |    26:29 |    20:16 |
| sys  | fedora-37           | root     |    26:25 |    18:51 |
| sys  | fedora-37-aarch64   | root     |    30:53 |    20:23 |
| sys  | fedora-36           | rootless |    24:13 |          |
| sys  | fedora-37           | rootless |    26:17 |    17:57 |
| bud  | fedora-37           | root     |    27:59 |    28:07 |

Compare against another PR from yesterday, with the currently-in-use VMs:

| type | distro              | user     |    local |   remote | container |
| ---- | --------            | ----     |    ----: |   -----: | --------: |
| int  | fedora-36           | root     |    29:15 |    30:44 |     28:32 |
| int  | fedora-37           | root     |    28:28 |    31:09 |     26:04 |
| int  | fedora-36           | rootless |    25:29 |          |
| int  | fedora-37           | rootless |    25:42 |          |
| sys  | fedora-36           | root     |    28:19 |    18:06 |
| sys  | fedora-37           | root     |    26:07 |    18:07 |
| sys  | fedora-37-aarch64   | root     |    29:01 |    19:35 |
| sys  | fedora-36           | rootless |    24:36 |          |
| sys  | fedora-37           | rootless |    24:48 |    15:43 |
| bud  | fedora-37           | root     |    26:48 |    26:33 |

Pretty much the same. Green light from me. (See [this comment](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/15272#issuecomment-1293458433) for why it's super-important to check timings when we update VMs).

And yes, I should write a tool to display better diffs, but that seems like a lot of work for not that much benefit.

> work for not that much benefit.

IMHO, new images can come in many times in a single PR and certainly once or twice per month.  I'd say that's in the window of frequent enough that forgetting to run timings could be of significant consequence.

I just opened https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/17084, which will enable generation of timings on `main`.  Assuming we can tabulate it, that would serve as a good benchmark for 'previous VM images', at least when examining a "new VM images" PR, no?

> @n1hility hey, could you look at the windows smoke test failure? It looks like podman machine init times out without any info printed.

Sure will check it out. At that location its failing before or during downloading the image, will see if other runs look like that.

The `machine podman fedora-37-aarch64 rootless host` is failing with a curl-getting-swagger error. 

I believe I "fixed" it, via Cirrus `Rerun in terminal` and immediately `chmod 777 /usr/local/bin`. The test is now proceeding. HOWEVER, if we merge this as it is, every future PR will also require this manual intervention. Possible solutions I see:
* find the step that got lost (where `/usr/local/bin` gets chmoded), restore it, rebuild VMs
* Add yet another kludge commit to this PR, in which something (`runner.sh`???) does the chmod.

In any event, this must not merge as it is, even if the `machine` and Windows tests pass, so I've added a whole bunch of red labels. Please remove once the `/usr/local/bin` problem is resolved.

> > work for not that much benefit.
> 
> IMHO, new images can come in many times in a single PR and certainly once or twice per month. I'd say that's in the window of frequent enough that forgetting to run timings could be of significant consequence.

I don't understand this, so I think maybe there's a misunderstanding.

I wrote "tool to display better diffs", by which I meant, "a tool that takes two sets of results from `cirrus-pr-timing`, from two different CI runs, compares the two, and shows the difference between them in minutes and seconds, so readers can easily see if a new set of VMs reintroduce a timing problem." Is that what you understood? I have a sense that maybe you understood my words to mean "automatically post the results of `cirrus-pr-timing` on PRs that update VM images"? Because that's not something within my skill set, hence is not anything I would even mention.

EDIT: Oh, I think I get it. Are you talking about the CSV timing results, the ginkgo ones? Yeah, completely different timings.

@Luap99 there was an update to the WSL implementation that breaks usage from local system services (where the agent runs). I need to come up with a workaround so for now I would just hardcode WINDOWS_AMI to use the previous suffix.

> I believe I "fixed" it, via Cirrus Rerun in terminal and immediately chmod 777 /usr/local/bin. The test is now proceeding. HOWEVER, if we merge this as it is, every future PR will also require this manual intervention. Possible solutions I see:


Wait a second, why the hell do we `chmod 777 /usr/local/bin`? This should 100% not be writeable by other users.
Second why the hell do the machine tests care about swagger? This is only needed for the API docs.
@cevich ^^

@cevich, this is the context: test was failing, curl-get-swagger. I have no idea why swagger is needed for podman-machine, but shrug, I don't want to find out right now. Rerun-in-terminal, chmod 777 /usr/local/bin, that fixed it. Again, I don't know or care why. All I know is, test was failing, curl-get-swagger. Maybe the VM image needs to have swagger in it? Maybe the VM image needs /usr/local/bin 777 ? Once again, I don't want to delve into it. This is probably an "oh that's easy" thing for you to figure out. If it isn't, then ok, I'll dive into it.
I may fixed it by not installing swagger when it is not needed, https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/16525/commits/18d15b5ec24ad90508dabe8cb7e18836a5299787

That's another possible approach, but it raises alarm bells because that line you touched (`install.swagger`) is very new, from 2022-10-14 #16161 and it looks like a deliberate addition: the PR name itself explicitly says "add swagger install". That means there was probably a Very Good Reason for adding it, and it might be a Bad Idea to remove it. @cevich PTAL at your earliest convenience.

/hold because this is a conflict that requires resolution.

> That's another possible approach, but it raises alarm bells because that line you touched (`install.swagger`) is very new, from 2022-10-14 #16161 and it looks like a deliberate addition: the PR name itself explicitly says "add swagger install". That means there was probably a Very Good Reason for adding it, and it might be a Bad Idea to remove it. @cevich PTAL at your earliest convenience.
> 
> /hold because this is a conflict that requires resolution.

The one task that should need it already installs it via the specific target, I see no reason why other tasks should suffer from flakes while downloading a binary they never need.
Since @cevich added this code, @cevich will resolve the question.

> The one task that should need it already installs it...no reason why other tasks should suffer

Yeah, I think this is the right approach.  I have no memory or answer to the WTF `/usr/local/bin` permissions.  But changing the Makefile and setup to only install swagger for swagger_task LGTM.

The de-sync of the windows image-suffix is unfortunate, and doing this has caused pretty ugly problems in the past.  Mainly to do with additional image-bound CI-breakage creeping in unnoticed.

@n1hility I know it's an evil question to ask, so I'll phrase it genteelly - Do you have a very-rough estimate how long a fix should take?  Are you thinking days or weeks or longer?

We also need to make sure no new release-branches are created with the non-synchronized images.  If that happens, then we get double-the-fun trying to re-sync them later on :scream:

> We also need to make sure no new release-branches are created

Ugh...this may actually be the case.   Well :hankey: 

@Luap99 let me guess, your preference is to get this PR merged before any new releases, ya?

To be clear, what I'm worried about is:

```
    # FIXME FIXME FIXME: hard coding the windows ID to use an older image
    # something is wrong with WSL with the new images, see https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/16525#issuecomment-1379890071
    WINDOWS_AMI: "win-server-wsl-c5069932136759296"
    # WINDOWS_AMI: "win-server-wsl-${IMAGE_SUFFIX}"
```

That's going to end up in a release-branch.  That makes me nervous.  Our image timestamping automation can handle it just fine, it's the human-maintainers I'm worried about :confounded: 

Context: It's both bad, and wrong (and often technically difficult) to update a CI VM-image set in release branches.  If we merge this, it means everyone agrees to live with the de-sync, basically forever.

Am I being paranoid, or is this a real problem we should consider?

> Am I being paranoid, or is this a real problem we should consider?

No this is a problem. I don't like it either, I don't think we have to merge it right now. The blocked PR already skipped the test anyway and was merged earlier so it should not block somebodies work, at least I am not aware of anything.

My only point is that it makes sense to test on more recent images. At this point I really do not believe the current images reflect the software on actual users systems any more and therefore we might miss critical bugs because of it. I know we have gating tests in fedora but they only cover a subset compared to upstream CI.

> No this is a problem. 

Okay, thanks for the sanity-check.  Let's wait for @n1hility to help fix Windows (what would we do without him!) and/or merge this PR after the release is branched.  Maybe the kernel bug will be resolved by then (one can wish).

> At this point I really do not believe the current images reflect the software on actual users systems any more

I 100% agree, the current images are based on a beta-build of F37 :confused: The good thing is, I think in a few weeks you've blown through image-update problems I was failing to fix for more than a month :grin:
@cevich It's a hairy problem, although I have a solution almost ready to go, just needs polishing. It will require PRs to images and podman to address.

@n1hility okay, thanks for the update and guesstimate.  Last I looked, you're in-the-clear for an automation_images PR, there's nothing else happening over there ATM.

This PR is the main one trying to update images for Podman CI, so we can update the ID here when it's ready.  Thanks again.

Rebased to include the windows fix, this should turn green now unless I am the most unluckiest person ever.
@cevich @edsantiago PTAL again
/lgtm
Not a single flake as far as I can tell, guess I am not so unlucky after all.
Nope, sometimes all it takes is persistence, and teamwork :+1:
